guest_name: Professor Steve Peters and Nir Eyal

key_insights.insight: Self-image strongly influences habits; changing your self-perception can alter your behavior.
key_insights.quote: If you define yourself image, you're actually programming your computer say this is normal Anything else isn't,and that will actually help your chimp to agitate which will then join forces and tidy the room.

key_insights.insight: Sometimes, significant change requires experiencing enough pain to motivate action.
key_insights.quote: Change happens when the pain of staying the same becomes greater than the pain of making a change.

key_insights.insight: Increase awareness of potential suffering by reflecting and contemplating negative consequences of inaction.
key_insights.quote: You can increase the concept of suffering by reflecting and thinking what would life be like if she left.

key_insights.insight: Commitment, rather than relying on motivation, is more effective for achieving goals.
key_insights.quote: If you look at the site neuroscience if you use commitment that means I remove my emotion and I plan on what I have to do and I get on with it socommitment there's a lot of evidence that that makes us succeed.

key_insights.insight: Mastering internal triggers is crucial for behavior change; understand the underlying sensation driving distractions.
key_insights.quote: The first step is mastering these internal triggers, figuring out why you feel this way, right? What is that underlying sensation?

key_insights.insight: Reframing internal triggers can transform discomfort into motivation by recognizing the positive value they signal.
key_insights.quote: Reframing it as not a negative,but something that happens not to me, but for me,that that sensation is a sign that I can listen to.

key_insights.insight: Distractions are managed with 4 steps: master internal triggers, make time for traction, hack back external triggers, prevent distraction with pacts.
key_insights.quote: So you master the internal triggers,you make time for traction,you hack back the external triggers. As the last line of defense,as the firewall against distraction,you're going to prevent distraction with a pact.

key_insights.insight: Internal triggers are the true root of most distractions, making external interventions only partially effective.
key_insights.quote: So step three is hacking back the external triggers...it's really only 10% of the problem, because 90% of our distractions begin from within.

key_insights.insight: Willpower is a finite resource that can be depleted; relying solely on it for habit change is unsustainable.
key_insights.quote: Willpower isn't just a skill, it's a muscle.Like the muscles in your arms or your legs,and it gets tired.And it gets tired as it's forced to work harder.

actionable_advice.advice: Change your self-image by reflecting on the person you want to be and consciously adopting that identity.
actionable_advice.how_to: Sit down and reflect, define the desired self-image, and consciously act in ways that align with that image.

actionable_advice.advice: Increase awareness of potential suffering to motivate change.
actionable_advice.how_to: Imagine the negative consequences of staying the same or not addressing a problem to feel the pain and trigger action.
Remove emotion from the process. Plan what needs to be done and simply execute the plan regardless of your feelings.

actionable_advice.advice: Practice the 10-minute rule to combat distractions.
actionable_advice.how_to: When you feel the urge to get distracted, tell yourself you can give in, but only in 10 minutes, then reassess the urge after 10 minutes.

actionable_advice.advice: Use effort pacts to prevent distractions.
actionable_advice.how_to: Introduce friction between yourself and potential distractions, such as using a timer to automatically shut off the internet router.

memorable_stories: The speaker recounts a personal experience using a timer to shut off the internet at night to improve his relationship with his wife.
The speaker describes a study involving cookies and radishes to illustrate the depletion of willpower.
contrarian_claims: Motivation is less effective than commitment for achieving goals.
Relying on 'just say no' for breaking habits can increase rumination and make the urge stronger.
External triggers are only 10% of the problem of distraction, with 90% coming from internal triggers.
episode_title: The Diary Of A CEO with Steven Bartlett - Moment 197: The 10-Minute Rule That Beats ANY Bad Habit & This Weird Trick Forms Good Habits Instantly! Professor Steve Peters & Nir Eyal
